Budget Guides 10 min read

Average Wedding Cost in Maryland: What Couples Spend in 2026

What does a wedding actually cost in Maryland? Real 2026 data from a Baltimore wedding planner covering venue, catering, photography, and staying under $20,000.

J

John

Baltimore Wedding Planner

Featured image, replace with real photo

The average wedding in Maryland costs $24,000, according to data from The Knot's 2025 Real Weddings Study. But that number hides a wide range. Couples I've worked with in Baltimore spend anywhere from $8,500 for an intimate 30-person ceremony to $45,000 for a full 150-person reception with all the extras.

As a Baltimore-based wedding planner who's helped 50+ Maryland couples stay on budget, the national average means very little here. What matters is where in Maryland you're marrying, how many guests you're inviting, and which vendors you're prioritizing. This guide breaks it all down with real numbers.

What Is the Average Wedding Cost in Maryland?

The average wedding cost in Maryland is $24,000 for couples who spend at the middle of the market. Budget-focused couples typically spend $8,000 to $15,000, while mid-range weddings run $15,000 to $30,000, and luxury celebrations start at $35,000.

Tier Maryland National Average
Budget$8,000 to $15,000$10,000 to $18,000
Mid-Range$15,000 to $30,000$18,000 to $35,000
Luxury$35,000 to $75,000+$40,000 to $100,000+

Maryland skews slightly below national averages because Baltimore and Annapolis have more mid-range venue options than cities like New York or San Francisco. That's good news if you want a real celebration without a six-figure price tag and you don't need to move mountains to pull it off.

What Does a Maryland Wedding Cost by Vendor Category?

Venue rental, catering, and photography eat up 65 to 75 percent of most Maryland wedding budgets. Everything else divides the remaining quarter.

Category Budget Mid-Range Luxury
Venue rental$500 to $2,500$3,000 to $8,000$8,000 to $20,000+
Catering (per person)$40 to $65$65 to $110$110 to $200+
Photography$1,500 to $2,500$2,500 to $4,500$5,000 to $10,000+
DJ or band$800 to $1,500$1,500 to $3,500$4,000 to $12,000
Florals and decor$500 to $1,500$1,500 to $4,000$5,000 to $15,000+
Wedding dress$500 to $1,200$1,200 to $3,000$3,000 to $10,000+
Wedding planner$500 to $1,500$1,500 to $2,800$3,000 to $8,000
Officiant$150 to $400$400 to $800$800 to $2,000

The 30-30-15-25 rule

I use this with every client: 30% venue, 30% catering, 15% photography, 25% everything else. On a $15,000 budget that's $4,500 for venue, $4,500 for catering, $2,250 for photos, $3,750 for the rest. It's not rigid, but it stops couples from burning 60% of their budget on a venue before they've priced a single other vendor.

Why Do Maryland Wedding Costs Vary So Much by Location and Season?

Maryland wedding costs shift by 20 to 40% based on location, season, and day of week. Knowing which levers to pull is how budget couples end up with weddings that look more expensive than they were.

Location impact:

Baltimore City venues run 10 to 25% higher than comparable spaces in Frederick, Carroll, or Harford County. Annapolis waterfront venues carry a premium for the views and year-round demand. Eastern Shore venues often deliver the best value, especially for weekday and off-season dates.

Guest count is the biggest variable:

Catering runs $65 to $110 per person at Maryland's mid-range market. A 30-person wedding costs $1,950 to $3,300 in food and drink. A 100-person wedding costs $6,500 to $11,000 for the same quality. Your guest list is the single biggest cost driver, which is why elopements and micro-weddings save couples $10,000 to $20,000 on average.

Season and day-of-week savings:

Peak season runs May through October. A venue quoting $5,000 for a September Saturday might rent for $2,500 on a January Friday. Most Maryland vendors charge 15 to 25% less for non-Saturday bookings. A Sunday afternoon wedding looks identical to a Saturday celebration in the photos, and it'll cost you significantly less to pull off.

Does Hiring a Maryland Wedding Planner Save You Money?

Yes, in most cases. Maryland vendors offer preferred pricing to planners who send them repeat business. I've negotiated 10 to 20% discounts on catering, photography packages, and florals for clients who wouldn't have gotten those rates on their own.

On a $20,000 Maryland wedding, a 15% average discount across three vendors saves $3,000. That's more than the cost of day-of coordination ($500 to $1,500) or even partial planning ($800 to $1,800).

Planners also catch expensive mistakes before they happen. Couples who sign venue contracts without reading the catering exclusivity clause, or who book photographers without confirming overtime rates, often face surprise charges of $500 to $2,000. I've seen it dozens of times.

How Do You Plan a Maryland Wedding Under $20,000?

A $20,000 wedding in Maryland is genuinely doable. I've done it many times. It requires smart priorities, not sacrifices.

  • Keep the guest list under 75. Every additional guest adds $70 to $120 in catering costs. Cutting 25 guests saves $1,750 to $3,000.
  • Book a non-Saturday date. Fridays and Sundays typically save $1,000 to $3,000 on venue costs for the same space.
  • Choose a venue that allows outside catering. All-inclusive venues lock you into their pricing. BYO-catering venues let you negotiate.
  • Skip the live band. A great Maryland DJ runs $1,000 to $1,800. A live band runs $5,000 to $10,000. The dance floor doesn't care.
  • Do florals selectively. Put your floral budget on the ceremony arch and bridal bouquet. Greenery and candles on tables cost a fraction of elaborate centerpieces.

What $15,000 looks like with smart planning:

  • Venue: $2,500 (weekday or off-season mid-range Baltimore venue)
  • Catering: $4,500 (50 guests at $90 per person)
  • Photography: $2,500 (solid mid-range Baltimore photographer)
  • DJ: $1,200
  • Florals: $1,000
  • Officiant: $350
  • Dress and attire: $1,200
  • Cake: $450
  • Day-of coordinator: $800
  • Tips, gifts, stationery: $800
  • Total: $15,300

Real wedding with professional vendors across all categories. Not a scaled-down version.

Working with a $15,000 to $20,000 Maryland wedding budget?

John specializes in making that budget look and feel like twice as much. Free 30-minute consultation to review your priorities and build a realistic plan.

Frequently Asked Questions About Maryland Wedding Costs

What's included in the average $24,000 Maryland wedding?

A $24,000 Maryland wedding typically includes venue rental ($4,000 to $6,000), catering for 75 to 100 guests ($6,000 to $9,000), a photographer ($2,500 to $3,500), a DJ ($1,500), florals ($1,500 to $2,500), and the rest split across the dress, officiant, cake, stationery, and tips. It's a mid-range celebration with professional vendors across every category.

Is it cheaper to get married in Baltimore or Annapolis?

Baltimore generally offers more budget-friendly venue options. Baltimore's industrial and arts district venues (Clipper Mill, Motor House) typically run $2,000 to $5,000 in venue rental. Annapolis waterfront venues often start at $4,000 to $7,000 for the same guest count. If you're set on Annapolis, a weekday or off-season date closes most of that gap.

What's the cheapest month to get married in Maryland?

January and February are the least expensive months. Venues discount by 20 to 40%, and most vendors are more flexible on pricing. March and November are also good value months. Peak demand runs June through October, when venues and photographers charge their highest rates of the year.

How much does an elopement cost in Maryland?

A Maryland park elopement runs $500 to $2,500 total. That covers the marriage license ($65 to $85), a park permit ($50 to $150), an officiant ($200 to $400), and a photographer ($1,000 to $1,500 for a 2-hour session). You can add florals, a cake, and a private dinner afterward and still keep the total under $4,000 in most cases.

Does hiring a wedding planner increase or decrease the total cost?

It depends on the service. Day-of coordination ($500 to $1,500) adds a line item but prevents expensive day-of mistakes. Full-service planning ($1,500 to $2,800) often pays for itself through vendor discounts and avoided costly decisions. Most of my full-service clients end up spending less total than they would have without planning help, because I catch budget problems early rather than after contracts are signed.

Data source: The Knot 2025 Real Weddings Study + Baltimore Wedding Planner client data (50+ Maryland weddings, 2022-2026).
